description: Formal-mathematics grounding for the math lens — the abstract reasoning fuel (analysis, algebra, probability, optimization, topology) the corpus already leans on, now routed to its lens.

entries.title: Banach fixed-point theorem
entries.summary: A contraction mapping T on a complete metric space (d(Tx,Ty) ≤ q·d(x,y) for some q<1) has a unique fixed point, and iterating x_{n+1}=T(x_n) converges to it geometrically. It is the engine behind existence/uniqueness of ODE solutions (Picard), Newton's method convergence, and value-iteration in dynamic programming — the same contraction argument that makes the Bellman operator converge.

entries.title: Eigenvalues, eigenvectors & the spectral theorem
entries.summary: An eigenvector v of A satisfies Av=λv: a direction A only scales. Eigenvalues govern the long-run behavior of x_{n+1}=Ax_n (stable iff all |λ|<1) and ẋ=Ax (stable iff all Re(λ)<0). The spectral theorem says a real symmetric matrix has an orthonormal eigenbasis with real eigenvalues — the foundation of PCA, the SVD, and quadratic-form optimization.

entries.title: Singular value decomposition
entries.summary: Every matrix A factors as UΣVᵀ with U,V orthogonal and Σ diagonal of non-negative singular values. The top-k truncation is the best rank-k approximation in Frobenius/spectral norm (Eckart–Young), making the SVD the mathematical core of PCA, latent-semantic indexing, recommender systems, and noise-robust pseudoinverses.

entries.title: Bayes' theorem
entries.summary: Bayes' theorem inverts conditional probability: P(H|E) = P(E|H)·P(H)/P(E). It updates a prior belief P(H) into a posterior given evidence, and is the formal basis of Bayesian inference, the Kalman filter's update step, naive-Bayes classifiers, and probabilistic reasoning under uncertainty.

entries.title: Central limit theorem & law of large numbers
entries.summary: The LLN says the sample mean of i.i.d. variables converges to the true mean; the CLT says the (normalized) sum of many independent finite-variance variables converges to a Gaussian regardless of the underlying distribution. Together they justify why averages are reliable and why the normal distribution is ubiquitous — the bedrock of statistical estimation and Monte-Carlo error bounds.

entries.title: Convex optimization & duality
entries.summary: A convex problem (convex objective over a convex set) has the defining property that any local minimum is global. The KKT conditions characterize optimality with constraints, and Lagrangian duality gives a lower bound that is tight under Slater's condition (strong duality). Convexity is what makes SVMs, LASSO, LQR, and MPC efficiently and reliably solvable.

entries.title: Gradient descent & convergence rates
entries.summary: Gradient descent steps x←x−η∇f. For an L-smooth convex f a step η≤1/L gives O(1/k) convergence; add strong convexity and it's linear (geometric). Momentum/Nesterov accelerate to O(1/k²); stochastic GD trades exactness for cheap iterations and underlies essentially all deep-learning training. The step size vs smoothness trade-off is the central tuning knob.

entries.title: Riemann zeta function & analytic continuation
entries.summary: ζ(s)=Σ n⁻ˢ converges for Re(s)>1 and extends by analytic continuation to the whole plane (bar s=1). Its Euler product over primes ties ζ to the distribution of primes; the Riemann Hypothesis — that all non-trivial zeros have Re(s)=1/2 — would pin the error term in the prime-counting function. A touchstone of how local data (primes) and a global analytic object interlock.

entries.title: Manifolds & tangent spaces
entries.summary: A manifold is a space that looks locally like Euclidean ℝⁿ (charts) while having global curvature/topology. At each point the tangent space linearizes the manifold, letting calculus run on curved spaces. This is the language of robot configuration spaces (a joint space is a torus, rotations are SO(3)), general relativity's spacetime, and manifold-learning in ML.

entries.title: Entropy & mutual information
entries.summary: Shannon entropy H(X)=−Σ p log p measures a distribution's uncertainty (and the optimal compressed bit-length); KL divergence D(p‖q) measures how far q is from p and is the loss minimized by maximum-likelihood; mutual information I(X;Y) measures shared information. These quantify compression limits, feature relevance, and the training objectives of most generative models.

entries.title: Graph theory: shortest paths & spectra
entries.summary: Graphs model relations; Dijkstra/Bellman–Ford solve shortest paths (the latter handling negative edges and underpinning distance-vector routing). The graph Laplacian's spectrum reveals connectivity (the number of zero eigenvalues = connected components) and drives spectral clustering and the message-passing of graph neural networks.

entries.title: Fourier transform
entries.summary: The Fourier transform decomposes a signal into frequencies; convolution in time becomes multiplication in frequency, which is why filtering, the FFT (O(n log n)), and PDE solutions are tractable. It is the bridge between the time-domain (state-space) and frequency-domain (transfer-function) views of a system and the front end of nearly all audio/image processing.

entries.title: Markov chains & stationary distributions
entries.summary: A Markov chain's next state depends only on the current one (memorylessness). An irreducible aperiodic chain converges to a unique stationary distribution π=πP regardless of start — the principle behind PageRank, MCMC sampling, and the state-transition models of reinforcement learning (MDPs add actions and rewards).

entries.title: Gödel incompleteness & computability
entries.summary: Gödel's first incompleteness theorem: any consistent formal system strong enough for arithmetic contains true statements it cannot prove. Turing's halting problem gives the computational mirror: no algorithm decides whether an arbitrary program halts. Together they bound what proof and computation can achieve — the ceiling under every formal/automated-reasoning system.
